Wolf et al. 2008 — responsive personalities in a spatially-explicit ABM

Source: vignettes/paper-wolf2008.Rmd
paper-wolf2008.Rmd

Spatially-explicit clade interpretation of Wolf, van Doorn & Weissing (2008) PNAS 105:15825–15830, “Evolutionary emergence of responsive and unresponsive personalities”. Captures the frequency-dependent benefit of sampling mechanism using clade’s grass economy and one-per-cell movement rule. The implementation is more compact than Wolf 2007’s (one trait, one module); the cross-context-consistency extension and the two-stage positive-feedback model from Wolf 2008 §3 are deferred follow-up work.

What the original paper proposes

Wolf 2008 extends Wolf 2007’s life-history mechanism with a different question: when should an agent be “responsive” (sample environmental state, then choose action conditional on it) versus “unresponsive” (use a fixed general-purpose action)? Two key ingredients:

  1. Negative frequency-dependent benefits of responsiveness (Methods Eq. 1). When responsiveness is rare, a responsive agent exploits cues with little competition — high benefit. When responsiveness is common, all responsives flock to the same resources — competition erodes the benefit. This produces stable coexistence of responsive and unresponsive types.

  2. Positive feedback on cost-of-responsiveness (Methods §3). An agent that was responsive in stage 1 pays a lower cost to be responsive in stage 2 (because it has already incurred the start-up costs). Even tiny cost-reductions produce strong cross-context consistency: agents responsive in one context tend to be responsive in others.

Wolf 2008 thus links the two empirical phenomena that defined the “animal personality” literature: coexistence of behavioural types (why polymorphism, not optimal monomorphism) and cross-context consistency (why agents are predictable across situations).

clade implementation

clade is spatially explicit. Wolf 2008 is non-spatial (50-island mean- field). We re-implement the frequency-dependent benefit mechanism using clade’s spatial machinery; the two-stage cross-context consistency extension is left for follow-up work.

One new heritable trait in [0, 1]:

  • responsiveness = Pr(this agent samples local state and overrides its action toward the richest free cardinal-neighbour cell on a given tick).

Sampling cost:

  • Per-tick energy drain responsiveness_cost paid by the agent whenever it samples (regardless of whether the resulting move succeeds or whether it improves on the current cell).

Frequency-dependence emerges from clade’s existing economy:

  • Many responsive agents → all flock to richest neighbours → handling- time depletes those cells → first-mover wins, others waste cost. Combined with one-per-cell movement (Phase 2), responsive agents at high density frequently find their richest target occupied and end up paying cost without moving.
  • Few responsive agents → rich cells stay rich → first-mover advantage yields net gain → trait selected for.

This is the same dE/dp_r < 0 condition Wolf states in Methods Eq. 1, realised via spatial competition rather than the abstract patch-choice denominator f_i = p_r·l_i + (1-p_r)·l̄.

See inst/julia/src/modules/responsiveness.jl.

What’s NOT implemented (deliberate)

  • Two-stage cross-context consistency (Wolf 2008 §3). Would need a per-agent times_responsive counter and a positive-feedback rule that lowers cost after prior responsiveness. clade has the infrastructure; this is a one-day follow-up task.
  • Per-resource (1+α·N_i) competition denominator. The same decision as Phase 3a (Wolf 2007). Would clean up the polymorphism result but isn’t essential for the headline mechanism.
  • Cost-of-uncertainty. Wolf 2008’s C is a single parameter; in reality cost might scale with environmental volatility, partial information, etc. Not modelled here.

The headline result 

library(clade)

s <- wolf2008_responsiveness_specs()
s$max_ticks   <- 2000L
s$random_seed <- 42L
env <- suppressWarnings(run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE))

Pull final-tick responsiveness:

recs  <- env$agents
alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),
                logical(1L))
resp  <- vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
                function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$responsiveness),
                numeric(1L))
mean(resp)   # selection moves the trait above the no-module baseline of 0.5

Typical values at this configuration (seed = 42):

Condition Mean responsiveness
No module (drift baseline) 0.500
Module on (selection) 0.669
Δ +0.169

The trait rises above the drift baseline — selection on responsiveness is real. The verified test at tests/testthat/test-responsive-personalities.R asserts this selection signal with seed 42.

Density-dependence sweep (Wolf’s headline mechanism)

Wolf’s prediction: responsiveness should DECREASE at higher density (more competition for rich cells erodes the benefit). The clade test:

sweep_density <- function(n_init, seed = 42L) {
  s <- wolf2008_responsiveness_specs()
  s$n_agents_init <- n_init
  s$max_agents    <- max(800L, 2L * n_init)
  s$max_ticks     <- 2000L
  s$random_seed   <- seed
  env <- suppressWarnings(run_alife(s, verbose = FALSE))
  recs  <- env$agents
  alive <- vapply(seq_along(recs), function(i) as.logical(recs[[i]]$alive),
                  logical(1L))
  if (sum(alive) < 5L) return(NA_real_)
  mean(vapply(seq_along(recs)[alive],
              function(i) as.numeric(recs[[i]]$responsiveness),
              numeric(1L)))
}

n_inits <- c(50L, 100L, 200L, 350L, 500L)
resp_means <- sapply(n_inits, sweep_density)
plot(n_inits, resp_means, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Initial population density",
     ylab = "Mean responsiveness at tick 2000",
     main = "Wolf 2008 density sweep — clade implementation")
abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "grey")

Expected pattern (per Wolf): monotonically decreasing. Observed in clade: non-monotonic, with strongest selection-for-responsiveness at intermediate density. At very high density, populations crash before the trait equilibrates. This is the gap between Wolf’s mean-field model and clade’s spatial implementation — see “honest discussion” below.

Honest discussion (continuing the Phase 4 theme)

Wolf 2008 reports clean coexistence of responsive + unresponsive types at intermediate C (Fig. 2c). clade’s implementation produces selection on the responsiveness trait but does NOT cleanly reproduce Wolf’s polymorphic equilibrium. Two reasons:

  1. No per-resource competition denominator (Wolf 2008 only — Wolf 2007’s denominator was added in Phase 6 of the 0.7.0 work; the responsiveness module here does not yet have an analogue). Wolf’s (1+α·N_i) in the patch-choice payoff is the mathematical machinery that makes per-agent benefit decline smoothly with the fraction of responders. clade’s spatial competition (handling time + one-per-cell) is more discrete: the first responsive agent to a rich cell gets all the benefit; later movers get nothing. This produces winner-take-all rather than smoothly frequency-dependent.

  2. Population dynamics. clade’s grass-economy regulator interacts with the responsiveness trait in ways Wolf’s model doesn’t see. At high density, the population crashes before the trait equilibrates — and trait-mean interpretations on a crashing population are unreliable (viability_report() flags this).

Like Phase 4 (Trivers): clade’s implementation captures the mechanism but the spatial setting changes the dynamics in ways that are themselves biologically interesting.

What’s left for follow-up

  • Two-context cross-consistency test (Wolf 2008 §3): pair the responsiveness module with the existing kin-altruism or hawk-dove modules; quantify whether agents responsive in foraging are also responsive in social contexts.
  • Positive-feedback cost reduction: implement times_responsive counter; cost in stage 2 = C × (1 − k × times_responsive).
  • Tunable per-resource denominator: optional (1+α·N_i) payoff scaling driven by per-cell agent density (cheap to compute via cell_to_agents-style enumeration that we already designed during Phase 2 even if Policy A wasn’t ultimately taken).
  • Compare with Wolf 2007: pair the responsiveness module with Wolf 2007 personality syndrome — does responsiveness correlate with the boldness-aggressiveness syndrome under both selection pressures?

References

  • Wolf, M., van Doorn, G.S. & Weissing, F.J. (2008) Evolutionary emergence of responsive and unresponsive personalities. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 105:15825–15830. doi:10.1073/pnas.0805473105.
  • DeWitt, T.J., Sih, A. & Wilson, D.S. (1998) Costs and limits of phenotypic plasticity. Trends in Ecology & Evolution 13:77–81.
  • Sih, A., Bell, A.M., Johnson, J.C. & Ziemba, R.E. (2004) Behavioral syndromes: an integrative overview. Q. Rev. Biol. 79:241–277.
  • Bell, A.M. (2007) Future directions in behavioural syndromes research. Proc. R. Soc. B 82:755–761.
